title: Consumers Report Stable Inflation Expectations, Lower Spending Growth Expectations

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The Federal Reserve Bank of New York released results from its January 2015 Survey of Consumer Expectations showing median one-year and three-year ahead inflation expectations remained stable at 2.9% and 3.0% respectively. Median household spending growth expectations fell sharply to 4.1% from 4.8% in December 2014, reaching the lowest level since the series began. The mean perceived probability of finding a job if one lost their current job increased to 53.3%, the highest since the series started.
key_points: Median one-year ahead inflation expectations: 2.9% (unchanged from prior month); three-year ahead: 3.0% (unchanged)
Median home price change expectations decreased to 3.4% from 3.6% in December 2014, the lowest since series inception
Median household spending growth expectations fell sharply to 4.1% from 4.8% in December 2014, reaching series low
Median earnings growth expectations held steady at 2.5% (high end of range since series began)
Mean perceived probability of finding a job rose to 53.3% from 51% in December 2014, highest since series started
Mean perceived probability of losing one's job remained at 14.5%, its lowest level since July 2013
why_it_matters: Consumer expectations for inflation, spending, and employment are leading indicators for economic activity and Fed policy decisions. The sharp decline in spending growth expectations and continued stability in inflation expectations provided input for the Fed's assessment of the economic outlook during this period. This type of survey data can inform expectations about consumer behavior and potential GDP growth.
macro_context: January 2015 SCE data showed stable inflation expectations at levels consistent with the Fed's 2% target, while spending growth expectations declined notably. Labor market sentiment showed improvement with increased job-finding confidence. These readings reflect consumer sentiment during a period when the Fed was approaching its first post-crisis rate hike.
market_structure_context: Note: SOURCE METADATA incorrectly labels topic as 'market structure' when content is consumer expectations survey data (macro catalysts). The source is a press release announcing January 2015 Survey of Consumer Expectations results, not a research paper. Consider correcting source_type to 'press_release' and event_type to 'data_release_other' for more accurate taxonomy.
